steps.code: struct Color { int r; int g; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Check-the-ampersand. Before tracing any struct-passing function, look at the parameter — does it have &? That one character decides everything.
No & on parameter c. This means pass-by-value: the function gets its own COPY of whatever struct is passed in. Draw two boxes — one for the caller's variable, one for the copy.
Caller box: bg.r = 200, bg.g = 50. Write these in your variable table now.
Call copies bg into c. Function box: c.r starts at 200, c.g starts at 50. Then c.r = 0 changes only the copy. The copy is destroyed when fade returns.
KEY: No & = copy = caller unchanged. bg.r is still 200. Output: 200. Final values: bg.r = 200, bg.g = 50.

steps.code: struct Size { int w; int h; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Check-the-ampersand. Read the function signature first — if there is no &, every write inside the function hits a throwaway copy.
No & on parameter s. Pass-by-value. The function will compute s.w / 2 = 40, but that result lives in the copy only.
Variable table: panel.w = 80, panel.h = 40. These are the caller's values.
Copy made: s.w = 80, s.h = 40. Body runs: s.w = 80 / 2 = 40. Copy is destroyed on return. panel is untouched.
KEY: No & = copy = caller unchanged. panel.w is still 80. Output: 80. Final values: panel.w = 80, panel.h = 40.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Check-the-ampersand. Find the & in the parameter list — it means the parameter IS the caller's variable, not a copy. One box, not two.
& found on parameter s. Pass-by-reference: s and the caller's variable share the SAME box. Any write to s.val writes directly into the caller.
Variable table: exam.val = 65, exam.max = 100.
s IS exam (same box). s.val = 100 writes directly into exam.val. Update table: exam.val = 100.
KEY: & = same box = caller changed. exam.val is now 100. Output: 100. Final values: exam.val = 100, exam.max = 100.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Write-a-ref-function pattern. Signature has three parts: (1) void return, (2) Type &param with the ampersand, (3) opening brace. The & is what makes writes persist in the caller.
Body: use param.field = value to set the field. Because of &, this writes directly into whatever the caller passed in.
KEY: Pattern is void name(Type &param) { param.field = value; }. The & makes the caller's struct change. Without it, you'd only change a throwaway copy.

steps.code: struct Coord { int lat; int lon; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Const-ref-check. When you see 'const' before the & parameter, the function can READ fields but CANNOT WRITE them. The caller is guaranteed unchanged.
const Coord &c: the & means no copy is made (efficient), but const means c.lat = ... would be a compiler error. Read-only access.
Variable table: home.lat = 42, home.lon = 71. These will NOT change because const prevents writes.
c IS home (& = same box), but const blocks writes. Function body only reads: prints 42 71. No variable table updates needed.
KEY: const & = read-only reference. No copy overhead, no mutation. home.lat still 42. Full output: 42 71 42. Final values: home.lat = 42, home.lon = 71 (unchanged).

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Write-a-const-ref-signature. The pattern has four parts: (1) void, (2) function name, (3) const Type &param. The const goes BEFORE the type.
Part 1-2: void return type + function name. The function only reads/prints, so void is correct.
Part 3: const Type &param. The const keyword promises the function will not modify the struct. The & avoids copying. Together: efficient read-only access.
KEY: Pattern is void fname(const Type &param). const = no writes allowed, & = no copy made. Use this whenever a function only needs to read or print a struct.

teachMe: + on strings concatenates (joins) them. "Hello" + " World" = "Hello World".

s = s + "def" appends "def" to s. "abc" + "def" = "abcdef", then s is overwritten.
Concatenation chains left to right: "Hi" + ", " + "Bob" + "!" = "Hi, Bob!".

clozeSentence: The ___ operator sends data to cout for printing
explanation: << is the insertion operator. cout << x; sends x's value to the output stream.

code: cout << "Line 1" << ___ << "Line 2";
clozeSentence: ___ creates a newline in the output
explanation: endl ends the current line and starts a new one. Line 1 and Line 2 appear on separate lines.

spec: Write one statement that prints the value of a variable called total to the terminal
explanation: cout << total; sends the value stored in total to the terminal output.

spec: Write one statement that prints the text "Result: " followed by the variable answer
explanation: Chain << to print a string literal and a variable together on one line.

steps.annotation: Trace: arr = {1, 2, 3}
slot is & alias to arr[0]; slot = 99 changes arr to {99, 2, 3}
Trace the array: arr = {10, 20, 30}
slot is & for arr[1]; slot = 50 changes arr to {10, 50, 30}

code:
int age;
___ >> age;
clozeSentence: ___ is the object used to read input from the user
explanation: cin (character input) reads data from the keyboard. cin >> age; waits for the user to type a number.

code:
int val;
cin ___ val;
clozeSentence: The ___ operator extracts input from cin into a variable
explanation: >> is the extraction operator. cin >> val; reads user input and stores it in val.

clozeSentence: To read the user's typed input into name, use ___
explanation: cin >> name; reads one word of input from the keyboard into name.

spec: Write one statement that reads an integer from the user into a variable called num
explanation: cin >> num; waits for the user to type a number and stores it in num.

spec: Write one statement that reads a string from the user into a variable called city
explanation: cin >> city; reads one word of text input from the keyboard into city.

steps.annotation: Trace: x = 5
y copies the current value of x; trace: x = 5, y = 5 (separate boxes)
b gets a copy of a (10); they are separate boxes now
Changing a to 20 does not touch b; final state: a = 20, b = 10
STRATEGY: Draw one box per variable. Write the value inside when it is assigned. m gets box with 8.
Copy rule: n = m reads m's CURRENT value (8) and writes it into n's own box. n = 8. The two boxes are independent from this point on.
Cross out 8 in m's box, write 20. n's box is untouched — assignment only changes the LEFT side.
KEY: Copies are snapshots — changing the original later never updates the copy. Final: m = 20, n = 8, output 8.

teachMe: y captured x's value (5) at the time of y = x. Mutating x later does not retro update y; they are independent boxes.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Draw a box for every variable the moment it appears. Write the initial value inside. p = 6.
q gets its own box. Copy p's current value (6) into it. q = 6.
Evaluate right side first: p + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8. Cross out 6 in p's box, write 8. q is still 6.
KEY: Each assignment is a fresh snapshot of the right side at that moment. q now reads p's updated value (8). Final: p = 8, q = 8.

teachMe: Each = creates a new step. b first captured 3, then captured 4 after a was updated.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Track each variable in its own box. Write the value when assigned, cross out old values on reassignment. r = 4.
s gets a box with initial value 0. Two boxes on the page now: r = 4, s = 0.
Read r's current value (4), write it into s's box. Cross out 0, write 4. s = 4.
KEY: Reassigning the source after the copy does not affect the copy. r changes to 9, s stays 4. Final: r = 9, s = 4.

teachMe: n copied m's value (8) at line 2. m changing to 20 later doesn't affect n.
b got a snapshot of a (3) at line 2. a later became 50, but b stays 3.
q copied p's value (12) at line 2. Changing q to 99 doesn't touch p — separate boxes.
y got a copy of x (7). Then x became 0 and y became 7+1=8. Each variable is independent after the copy.
d copied c (15), then e copied d (15). Changing c to 100 doesn't propagate — each copy is a one-time snapshot.
s = r + 1 evaluates to 4+1=5 at assignment time. r later changing to 30 doesn't recalculate s.

clozeSentence: b holds ___ because it copied a's value at assignment time, not after
explanation: b = a copies the current value of a (5). a changing to 99 later has no effect on b.

clozeSentence: To make y hold a snapshot of x's current value, write y = ___
explanation: int y = x copies x's value (10) into y. After this, x and y are independent variables.

spec: Declare int z and assign it a copy of variable w
explanation: int z = w creates z and copies w's current value into it. No link remains between them.

spec: Given int a = 6; write a line that stores a's value in a new int b
explanation: int b = a takes a one-time snapshot of a (6). Later changes to a won't affect b.

teachMe: y was reassigned in line 3, capturing x's then current value (7). Line 4 changes x but does not affect y.

steps.annotation: Trace: arr = {0, 7, 0}; update slot 1 in your table
arr[1] is 7, so arr[2] = 7+1 = 8; trace: arr = {0, 7, 8}
Trace: arr changes from {5, 5, 5} to {10, 5, 5}
STRATEGY: Draw a row of numbered boxes for the array — data[0]=0, data[1]=0, data[2]=0. Index starts at 0.
Go to index 2 (third box). Cross out 0, write 5. Other slots unchanged. Array: [0, 0, 5].
KEY: Read the value at the index on the right side FIRST — data[2] is 5, so 5 + 3 = 8. Write 8 into data[0]. Final array: [8, 0, 5].

teachMe: arr[2] = arr[1] + 1 reads arr[1]'s current value (7), so arr[2] becomes 8. arr[0] is never touched.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Draw 4 boxes labelled [0] through [3]. Fill them: nums[0]=10, nums[1]=20, nums[2]=30, nums[3]=40.
Read nums[3] first — its value is 40. Write 40 into nums[1]. Cross out 20, write 40. Array: [10, 40, 30, 40].
KEY: Copying from an index takes a snapshot — later changing the source index does not undo the copy. nums[3] becomes 0, but nums[1] stays 40. Final: [10, 40, 30, 0].

teachMe: arr[1] reads the second element (index 1). Arrays are 0-indexed, so {10,20,30} has arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30.
v[0] = 1 overwrites only slot 0 (was 5, now 1). v[3] = 9 overwrites only slot 3. Slots 1 and 2 stay at 5.
d[0]+d[2] = 2+6 = 8. This is written into d[1], replacing the old value 4. Only slot 1 changes.
nums[2] reads the value 9 from index 2. That value is copied into x. The array is unchanged.
a[2] is overwritten to 0. Then a[0] = a[3] copies value 4 into slot 0. Each assignment targets exactly one slot.
w[0] + 5 reads the current value 100 and adds 5. The result 105 is stored back into w[0]. Other slots unchanged.

clozeSentence: To print 30 (the third element), the index is ___
explanation: Arrays are 0-indexed. arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30. Index 2 gives the third element.

clozeSentence: To change the last element of a 4-element array, use index ___
explanation: A 4-element array has indices 0,1,2,3. The last valid index is size-1 = 3.

spec: Set the second element of array scores to 42
explanation: The second element has index 1. scores[1] = 42 overwrites that one slot.

spec: Read element at index 0 from array data and store it in int first
explanation: data[0] reads the first element. That value is copied into the new variable first.

teachMe: v[0] = v[3] copies v[3]'s value (4) into v[0]. v[3] is then set to 0. Final array: [4, 2, 3, 0].

steps.annotation: Trace each iteration: i=1 sum=1, i=2 sum=3, i=3 sum=6, i=4 sum=10
Final state after loop: sum = 10 (1+2+3+4)
Trace each pass: i=1 product=1, i=2 product=2, i=3 product=6
Final result: product = 6 (which is 1 * 1 * 2 * 3)
STRATEGY: Make a two-column table — one column for the loop variable, one for the accumulator. Write each row as you go. acc = 0.
k starts at 1. Each iteration: check k <= 3, run body, then k++. Trace row by row: k=1 (body runs), k=2 (body runs), k=3 (body runs), k=4 (condition fails, loop ends).
Iteration 1: acc = 0+1 = 1. Iteration 2: acc = 1+2 = 3. Iteration 3: acc = 3+3 = 6. Always use the CURRENT acc value, not the original.
KEY: The loop variable increments ONE PAST the boundary before stopping. Final: acc = 6, k = 4 (exit value). Count iterations by listing each k that enters the body: 1, 2, 3 = 3 iterations.

teachMe: i takes values 1, 2, 3, 4 (loop body runs 4 times). i becomes 5 when the cond first fails. sum accumulates 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Build an iteration table with columns for the loop variable and the accumulator. Update both each pass. count = 0.
j starts at 0. Condition is j < 4 (strict less-than). Body runs for j = 0, 1, 2, 3. When j becomes 4, condition fails and loop exits.
Each iteration adds a constant 3. Trace: j=0 count=3, j=1 count=6, j=2 count=9, j=3 count=12. Four iterations total.
KEY: For a constant-add loop, total = iterations x amount. 4 iterations x 3 = 12. Final: count = 12, j = 4 (exit value).

teachMe: Iteration by iteration: i=1 sum=0+1=1, i=2 sum=1+2=3, i=3 sum=3+3=6, i=4 sum=6+4=10. Loop exits when i becomes 5 (5<=4 is false).
Iteration by iteration: i=1 product=1*1=1, i=2 product=1*2=2, i=3 product=2*3=6. This computes 3! = 6.
Iteration by iteration: i=0 total=10-2=8, i=1 total=8-2=6, i=2 total=6-2=4. Loop runs 3 times (i goes 0,1,2). Exits when i=3 (3<3 is false).
Countdown loop: j=5 count=1, j=4 count=2, j=3 count=3. Exits when j=2 (2>=3 is false). Runs 3 iterations.
Doubling each iteration: i=0 val=2*2=4, i=1 val=4*2=8, i=2 val=8*2=16, i=3 val=16*2=32. Four doublings from 2 gives 32.
Step size is 2: k=2 s=0+2=2, k=4 s=2+4=6, k=6 s=6+6=12. Sums even numbers 2+4+6=12. Exits when k=8 (8<=6 is false).

clozeSentence: The ___ operator accumulates i into sum each iteration
explanation: sum = sum + i adds each value of i (1, 2, 3) to the running total, producing 6.

clozeSentence: To run the loop for i = 0,1,2,3,4 the condition operator is ___
explanation: i < 5 is true for i = 0,1,2,3,4 and becomes false when i = 5, giving exactly 5 iterations.

clozeSentence: For multiplication accumulation, the initial value of product must be ___
explanation: Multiplying by 1 doesn't change the first value. Starting at 0 would make product stay 0 forever.

spec: Write a for loop header that runs i from 0 to 4 (inclusive)
explanation: for (init; condition; step) — i starts at 0, loop runs while i <= 4, i increments by 1 each time.

spec: Write a for loop header that counts j down from 10 to 1 (inclusive)
explanation: j starts at 10, continues while j >= 1, and decrements each iteration. Runs 10 times.

spec: Write the loop body line that adds the current element arr[i] to a running total variable sum
explanation: sum = sum + arr[i] reads the current array element and adds it to the accumulator each iteration.

teachMe: Body runs for i = 0, 1, 2 (3 times); total becomes 6. Loop exits when i = 3 makes cond false.

prompt: Write the for loop header: for (int i = 0; i < count; i++)

variants.prompt: Write the for loop header: for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
Write the for loop header: for (int j = 0; j < 10; j++)

steps.annotation: Evaluate: 7 > 5 is true, so take the if branch
Trace: label = 1 (the else branch is never entered)
3 > 5 is false, so skip the if body and enter the else block
Trace: result = 0 (the else branch was taken because condition was false)
STRATEGY: Evaluate the condition BEFORE reading either branch. Substitute the variable's current value into the condition, get true or false, then only trace the winning branch. val = 10.
Substitute: 10 > 8 is TRUE. The if-branch will run. Skip the else-branch entirely — do not trace any lines inside it.
KEY: Only one branch ever executes. Condition was true so tag = 1. The else line (tag = 2) never runs. Final: val = 10, tag = 1.

teachMe: n is 7, so n > 5 is true. The if branch runs and label = 1. The else branch is skipped - label never sees value 2.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Substitute the variable value into the if-condition to decide true/false FIRST, then trace only the branch that matches. score = 2.
Substitute: 2 > 5 is FALSE. Skip the if-branch (grade = 50 never runs). Jump to the else-branch.
KEY: When the condition is false the else-branch runs. grade = 100. The if-branch is dead code for this input. Final: score = 2, grade = 100.

teachMe: x is 7. Condition 7 > 10 is false, so the else branch runs and prints "small". The if body is skipped entirely.
temp is 35. Condition 35 >= 30 is true, so the if branch runs: msg = "hot". The else branch is skipped.
n is 0. First check: 0 > 0 is false. Second check: 0 < 0 is false. Falls through to else: label = "zero".
score is 85. First: 85 >= 90 is false. Second: 85 >= 80 is true, so grade = "B". The else is never reached.
age is 17. Condition 17 >= 18 is false, so the else branch runs: allowed = false. cout prints 0 for false.
a and b are both 4. First: 4 > 4 is false. Second: 4 < 4 is false. Falls to else: prints "tie".

clozeSentence: For x=3 to print "less", the comparison operator must be ___
explanation: 3 < 5 is true, so the if branch runs. Other operators like > or == would give false here.

clozeSentence: The keyword that introduces the fallback branch is ___
explanation: else provides the fallback when the if condition is false. 10 > 10 is false, so else runs.

spec: Write an if condition that checks whether variable age is greater than or equal to 18
explanation: if (age >= 18) evaluates to true when age is 18 or more, false otherwise.

spec: Write an else-if that checks whether score is greater than or equal to 50
explanation: else if adds another condition check. It only runs if all previous conditions were false.

teachMe: x is 3, so x > 5 is false. The else branch runs; result = 200. The if branch is skipped.

steps.annotation: Call square: enter new frame with x=5, compute 5*5=25, return 25
Back in main: r = 25 (the returned value); prints 25
Enter double_it: n=7, compute 7*2=14, return 14 to the caller
Back in main: val = 14 (the returned result); output is 14

teachMe: Outer: 5 > 0 is true, enter outer if. Inner: 3 > 0 is true, enter inner if. Prints "both pos". Both else branches skipped.
Outer: 8 > 0 is true, enter outer if. Inner: -2 > 0 is false, so inner else runs. Prints "a only".
Outer: -1 >= 0 is false, so the outer else runs immediately. The inner if/else is never reached. Prints "negative".
Outer: 25 > 20 is true. Inner: raining is true. Prints "warm rain". Two true conditions = deepest if body.
Outer: 10 > 5 is true, enter outer if. Inner: 0 > 5 is false, so inner else runs. Prints "tall".

clozeSentence: The inner check is a nested ___ statement inside the outer if
explanation: A nested if inside another if creates two levels of branching. The inner if only runs if the outer condition was true.

clozeSentence: The inner ___ runs when a > 0 is true but b > 0 is false
explanation: The inner else is the fallback for the inner if. It only executes when the outer if passed but the inner if failed.

spec: Write a nested if inside an outer if(x > 0) block that checks if y is also greater than 0
explanation: Nested ifs check conditions in sequence. The inner if only runs when the outer condition is already true.

steps.annotation: Pass by value: f_val gets a copy; n stays 5 after the call
Pass by reference: f_ref shares n via &; n becomes 99 after the call
Value pass: copy is zeroed but a stays 10 in main
Reference pass: a itself becomes 0 because & shares the same box
STRATEGY: Check the parameter for &. No & means pass-by-value — draw a SEPARATE box for the parameter. Changes inside the function cannot reach the caller's variable. w = 3.
Call g(w): a is a VALUE parameter (no &). Draw a new box labelled 'a', copy w's value (3) into it. Two independent boxes: w = 3, a = 3.
Cross out 3 in a's box, write 50. w's box is untouched because a is a separate copy.
KEY: No & = copy. The function mutated its own copy (a = 50) but w is still 3. Output: 3. Final: w = 3.

teachMe: x is a value parameter - separate box from n. Mutating x to 99 does not affect n. n remains 5; output is 5.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Check the parameter for &. The & means pass-by-reference — the parameter is an ALIAS for the caller's variable, not a separate box. z = 7.
Call h(z): b has &, so b IS z. Do NOT draw a new box — write 'b' as a second label on z's box. One shared box.
b = 42 means z = 42 because they share the same box. Cross out 7, write 42.
KEY: & = alias (same box). The function's write to b directly changed z. Output: 42. Final: z = 42.

teachMe: add5 takes x by value — a copy. x becomes 15 inside the function, but n in main stays 10. No ampersand = no effect on caller.
add5 takes x by reference (&x). x IS n — same box, two labels. x + 5 = 15 writes directly into n. Caller sees the change.
triple takes val by value (no &). val becomes 12 inside the function, but num stays 4. Pass-by-value = separate copy.
triple takes val by reference (&val). val and num are the same box. val * 3 = 12 writes directly to num.
reset takes x by value. x = 0 only zeroes the local copy. score in main is still 88. Without &, the caller is untouched.
halve takes v by reference (&v). v IS amount — one box, two names. v / 2 = 10 updates amount directly.

clozeSentence: To make inc modify the caller's variable, put ___ before the parameter name
explanation: The & makes x a reference to the caller's variable. Without it, x is a local copy and changes don't propagate back.

clozeSentence: n prints ___ because zero takes x by value (no ampersand)
explanation: Without &, x is a copy. Setting x = 0 doesn't affect n. n stays 5.

spec: Write a function header for void doubleIt that takes an int by reference named val
explanation: int &val means val is a reference to the caller's variable. Any change to val changes the caller's int.

spec: Write a function header for void inspect that takes an int by value named num
explanation: No & means num is a copy. The function can read it but changes won't affect the caller.

teachMe: x is an &-parameter - same box as n. Mutating x to 99 IS mutating n. After f returns, n = 99; output is 99.

steps.annotation: Start max at first element (3.2); compare against remaining elements
Trace: i=1 7.1>3.2 yes max=7.1, i=2 no, i=3 9.4>7.1 yes max=9.4, i=4 no
Final max_val = 9.4, the largest element in the array
Start min at first element (5); scan the rest for anything smaller
Trace: i=1 2<5 yes min=2, i=2 8<2 no, i=3 1<2 yes min=1; final min=1
STRATEGY: array-max pattern. Initialise array, then track running maximum starting from index 0. Compare each subsequent element.
max_val = 4.0 (copy of nums[0]). This is our running maximum.
i=3: nums[3]=1.5 > 8.0? No. i=4: nums[4]=6.0 > 8.0? No. Loop ends.
KEY: max_val = 8.0. Only one update occurred (at i=2). The largest element was in the middle of the array.

teachMe: Init max_val with nums[0] = 3.2. i=1: 7.1 > 3.2 -> max_val = 7.1. i=2: 5.0 not > 7.1 -> no update. i=3: 9.4 > 7.1 -> max_val = 9.4. i=4: 2.8 not > 9.4 -> no update. Final max_val = 9.4.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: array-max with 4 elements. Init array, track running max from v[0], compare v[1]..v[3].
max_val = 6.3 (copy of v[0]).
i=3: v[3]=4.8 > 9.2? No. Loop ends.
KEY: max_val = 9.2. One update at i=2. First element was not the max.

teachMe: Init max_val = 2.1. i=1: 8.5 > 2.1 -> update to 8.5. i=2 and i=3: not > 8.5 -> no update. Final max_val = 8.5.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: ascending-ish array means multiple updates expected. Track each comparison carefully.
max_val = 1.2 (smallest value, so many updates ahead).
KEY: max_val = 7.0. Three updates (i=1,2,4). Last element was the largest.

teachMe: Init 0.5 -> 1.8 -> 2.1 -> (0.9 skipped) -> 3.0. Final max_val = 3.0.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: full trace of who_am_i — it finds the max of numbers[]. Init mystery = numbers[0] = 4.5. Trace ALL iterations i=1..4.
i=4: 1.9 > 10.3? No, skip. Loop ends.
KEY: data.mystery = 10.3. Two updates (i=1, i=3). The function finds the maximum value in the array.

teachMe:

i=1: 7.1 > 3.2 OK -> 7.1
i=2: 5.0 > 7.1 X (no update)
i=3: 9.4 > 7.1 OK -> 9.4
i=4: 2.8 > 9.4 X (no update)

i=4: 81.7 > 95.5? No, skip. Loop ends.
KEY: data.mystery = 95.5. Only one update at i=1. Early max means no further updates.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: partial trace — stop BEFORE iteration i=3 executes. Only trace i=1 and i=2. Init mystery = 77.0.
i=2: 61.5 > 93.2? No, skip. Now i increments to 3 — STOP here (i==3).

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: full trace — max appears at index 2. Init mystery = 5.5. Trace ALL iterations i=1..4.
i=4: 4.6 > 9.1? No, skip. Loop ends.

i=2: 10.4 > 6.6? Yes -> mystery = 10.4. Now i increments to 3 — STOP here (i==3).

i=3: 3.1 > 6.8 X (no update)

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: partial trace — stop BEFORE iteration i=3 executes. Only trace i=1 and i=2. Init mystery = 7.3.
i=2: 8.6 > 7.3? Yes -> mystery = 8.6. Now i increments to 3 — STOP here (i==3).

Q1 sim variant 5, partial stop at i==3 (BEFORE iter i=3 runs; iters i=1, i=2 already complete).
init: 5.0
i=1: 4.2 > 5.0 X (no update)
i=2: 6.8 > 5.0 OK -> 6.8

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: full trace — strictly ascending array means every iteration updates. Init mystery = 2.0. Trace ALL iterations i=1..4.
KEY: data.mystery = 10.0. Four updates (every iteration). Ascending order = max updates.

teachMe:
Q1 sim variant 6, full trace.

clozeSentence: The max finder initialises mx with vals[___], the first element

explanation: Since mx is initialised to vals[0], comparing vals[0] to itself is redundant. Start at 1 to skip it.

code: if (vals[i] ___ mx) mx = vals[i];
clozeSentence: The comparison operator ___ checks if the current element beats the running maximum
explanation: vals[i] > mx is true only when the current element is strictly larger than the best seen so far.

steps.annotation: & means p is an alias for a; changes inside shift affect a in main
Trace: a.x was 3, now 4; a.y was 4, now 5 (both mutated via &)
Trace: box.w was 10, now 15; box.h was 20, now 25 (both via &)
Prints 15 25; the struct fields were mutated through the reference

steps.code: struct Pos { int r; int c; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: struct passed by reference (&). The function parameter p and the caller variable loc share the same memory. Any write through p changes loc directly.
loc.r = 5. Initialise the struct field r.
loc.c = 8. Initialise field c. loc is now {r=5, c=8}.
Inside nudge: p is &-bound to loc. p.r = 5 + 1 = 6. This writes directly to loc.r.
p.c = 8 + 1 = 9. Writes directly to loc.c.
KEY: loc = {r=6, c=9}. Both fields incremented by 1 because & means one box, two labels.

teachMe: p is &-bound to a; one struct box, two labels (a and p). Mutations through p update a directly. Final a = {4, 5}.

steps.code: struct Frame { int wd; int ht; };
steps.annotation: STRATEGY: struct passed by reference (&). The parameter f and caller variable panel share one memory box. Writes through f update panel.
panel.wd = 3. Initialise width field.
panel.ht = 7. panel is now {wd=3, ht=7}.
Inside stretch: f is &-bound to panel. f.wd = 3 * 2 = 6. Writes directly to panel.wd.
f.ht = 7 * 2 = 14. Writes directly to panel.ht.
KEY: panel = {wd=6, ht=14}. Both fields doubled because & shares the whole struct box.

teachMe: nudge takes Point by reference (&p). p IS pt. p.x+1=4 and p.y+2=9 write directly into pt's fields. Caller sees both changes.
scale takes Rect by reference. r IS box. r.w*2=10 and r.h*2=6 modify box directly. Both fields persist after the call.
addBonus takes Score by value (no &). s is a copy. s.pts becomes 60 inside the function, but game.pts stays 50. No ampersand = no effect on caller.
flip takes Vec by reference. tmp saves dx (4). dx gets dy (-1). dy gets tmp (4). The swap modifies dir directly via &.
zeroA takes Pair by reference. p.a = 0 sets data.a to 0 via &. data.b is untouched at 11. Reference shares the whole struct but only modified fields change.

clozeSentence: To make move modify the caller's struct, the parameter needs ___
explanation: Pos &p makes p a reference to the caller's Pos. Without &, p is a copy and changes don't persist.

clozeSentence: To zero the quantity field, access it via it.___
explanation: The dot operator accesses struct fields. it.qty = 0 sets the qty field to 0 through the reference.

clozeSentence: info.max prints ___ because resetMax takes Stats by value
explanation: No & means s is a copy. s.max = 0 only affects the copy. info.max stays 99.

spec: Write a function header for void resetPos that takes a Point struct by reference named p
explanation: Point &p makes p a reference to the caller's Point struct. Any field changes via p persist after the call.

spec: Inside a function that has Rect &r, write a line that doubles the w field
explanation: r.w accesses the w field through the reference. Since r is &, this modifies the caller's struct directly.

teachMe: & on Box param shares the whole struct box. b.w and b.h writes are caller's bx.w and bx.h writes. Final bx = {10, 20}.

steps.annotation: Each iteration compares numbers[i] to current mystery (running max)
Only updates mystery when a larger value is found; trace: 2.0->8.0 (final)
Trace the accumulator each pass: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10, 10+5=15
mystery accumulates the total; after the loop it holds 15 (the sum)
STRATEGY: inline max-find (no loop). Three explicit if-checks compare each element to the running mystery value. Walk each if in order.
mystery = 5.5 (copy of numbers[0]). This is the starting candidate for maximum.
numbers[1] = 3.0. Is 3.0 > 5.5? No -> skip. mystery stays 5.5.
numbers[2] = 8.2. Is 8.2 > 5.5? Yes -> mystery = 8.2.
KEY: d.mystery = 8.2. One update (numbers[2]). Same max-finding logic as the loop version, just unrolled.

teachMe: mystery starts at nums[0]=2.5. i=1: nums[1]=6.1>2.5 true, mystery=6.1. i=2: nums[2]=4.3>6.1 false. Max is 6.1. Struct field + array indexing combined.
biggest starts at 1.1. i=1: 3.8>1.1 true, biggest=3.8. i=2: 2.9>3.8 false. i=3: 5.4>3.8 true, biggest=5.4. Two updates needed across the struct's array.
top starts at items[0]=7.7. i=1: 5.2>7.7 false. i=2: 3.9>7.7 false. First element was already max. top never updates. Descending data = zero updates.
best starts at 4.0. i=1: 4.5>4.0 true, best=4.5. i=2: 3.2>4.5 false. i=3: 4.8>4.5 true, best=4.8. i=4: 4.1>4.8 false. Max is 4.8 via chained struct.array access.

clozeSentence: To access the array inside the struct, use d.___ with an index
explanation: d.nums[1] chains struct field access (d.nums) with array indexing ([1]) to read the second element.

clozeSentence: mx should be initialised to ___ (the first array element's value)
explanation: mx starts at nums[0] = 1.0. The loop then compares from index 1 onward to find the true max.

spec: Given struct Data with field double nums[3], write the expression that reads the second element
explanation: d.nums accesses the array field, then [1] indexes into it. Chained access: struct dot field then bracket index.

spec: Write the if statement that updates d.best when d.scores[i] is larger
explanation: Compare the struct's array element to the struct's scalar field. Update the scalar only when the element is bigger.

teachMe: Initialize d.mystery from d.numbers[0] = 4.0. Test d.numbers[1] (7.5) > 4.0 -> true, update mystery to 7.5. Test d.numbers[2] (2.0) > 7.5 -> false, skip. Final mystery = 7.5.

steps.annotation: To write a struct, start with struct followed by the type name and {
List each field with its type and name, one per line
Close with }; to complete the custom type definition
Begin with the keyword struct, then your chosen type name, then {
Add each field that describes one piece of data about the entity
Finish the definition with }; so the compiler registers the new type
STRATEGY: Every struct starts with 'struct' then the name ending in _data, then open brace.
Declare each field on its own line: type then name then semicolon. int for whole numbers.
string for text fields. Each field is indented inside the braces.
List all fields the spec asks for, one per line, matching the required types exactly.
KEY: Pattern is struct name_data { type field; type field; ... }; -- closing brace PLUS semicolon.

Third field: match exactly what the spec asks for. Don't add extra fields.

string field for text. Each field indented and on its own line.

clozeSentence: The keyword ___ begins a struct definition
explanation: Every struct definition starts with the keyword struct, then the name, then { fields };

clozeSentence: After struct, the ___ names this data type
explanation: The struct name goes between struct and the opening brace — it becomes the type name you use later

clozeSentence: A struct definition ends with ___ to close the body
explanation: Struct body closes with }; — the semicolon after the closing brace is required in C++

clozeSentence: After the struct name, ___ opens the body
explanation: The opening brace { begins the struct body where fields are declared

clozeSentence: After the closing brace }, a ___ is required
explanation: C++ requires a semicolon after the closing brace of a struct definition — }; not just }

steps.annotation: The struct body opens with {
The closing }; with semicolon is mandatory; without ; the code will not compile
Opening brace starts the list of fields
The }; is essential; leaving off the semicolon causes a compile error

teachMe: Brace init: Point p = {4, 9} sets p.x to 4 and p.y to 9, in field declaration order.
Brace init fills fields in order: first value goes to label, second to count.
Brace init: {3.14, "cm"} fills value then unit in declaration order.
Three fields initialized in order: name gets "Li", points gets 88, avg gets 92.5.
Brace init: {2, 5} sets row=2, col=5. Output is 2 * 5 = 10.

steps.annotation: Pick int for IDs and counts, string for text, double for monetary amounts
The spec says "title" is text, so string is the right type choice
Prices need decimals, so double (not int) is the correct type
Use int for whole number data like years and counts
Use string when the spec describes text data like names or titles
Use double for any value that might have a decimal component

teachMe: Dot access: p.x reads/writes field x of struct p. 3 + 7 = 10.
Dot access sets each field. b.w * b.h = 4.5 * 2.0 = 9.
Dot access works for string and int fields. a.name = "pen", a.qty = 5.
You can update a field: c.val = c.val + 5 reads 10, adds 5, stores 15.
One field can be computed from another: t.f = t.c * 1.8 + 32 = 100 * 1.8 + 32 = 212.
Struct fields work in any expression: 8 - 3 = 5, 8 % 3 = 2.
Dot access assigns and reads mixed types: string name and double gpa.
Init sets v.x=1, v.y=2. Then dot access updates: 1+10=11, 2+20=22.

clozeSentence: The ___ operator accesses a field of a struct variable
explanation: Dot operator: p.x accesses field x of struct variable p

clozeSentence: To assign to a field: d___ = value
explanation: Dot access for assignment: d.val = 42 sets the val field of d

clozeSentence: To print the text field: cout << c.___
explanation: c.make reads the string field — outputs Toyota

clozeSentence: Use the variable name ___ with dot to access each field
explanation: s.max and s.min — the struct variable name goes before the dot

spec: Given struct Pos { int x; int y; }; and Pos p;  Write two lines: set p.x to 6 and p.y to 11
explanation: Dot access for assignment: p.x = 6; p.y = 11;

spec: Given struct Product { string name; double price; }; and Product p;  Write two lines: set p.name to "milk" and p.price to 3.50
explanation: Dot access: p.name = "milk"; p.price = 3.50;

spec: Given struct Reading { int sensor_id; double value; string unit; }; and Reading r;  Write three lines assigning: sensor_id = 7, value = 23.5, unit = "C"
explanation: Dot access for each field: r.sensor_id = 7; r.value = 23.5; r.unit = "C";

steps.annotation: Fields can appear in any order; the compiler does not enforce a sequence
Whether id is first or last does not change how the struct works
Putting rating first is perfectly valid; field order does not affect behavior
The compiler accepts any arrangement of fields within the struct

teachMe: Passing struct to function: area(box) copies box into parameter r. r.w * r.h = 5 * 3 = 15.
show(a) copies struct a into parameter s. Inside show, s.name is "Jo" and s.mark is 85.
sum(p) copies p into v. v.x + v.y = 4 + 6 = 10.
print_price(item) copies the struct. p.amount=9.99, p.currency="AUD".
circumference(ring) copies ring into c. 2 * 3.14 * 5.0 = 31.4.

steps.annotation: void means this function reads data but returns nothing

steps.annotation: Type list[] passes the struct array so the function can fill it
The function writes into the caller array through the passed reference
Type list[] in the parameter passes the caller array for the function to fill
Each loop iteration populates one struct element in the array
STRATEGY: Signature pattern is void read_X(Type &list[], int count). The & makes it pass-by-reference so changes persist.
Loop from 0 to count-1. This visits every element in the array that needs to be filled.
Read each field with cin >> list[i].field; -- one cin line per field. Start with the first field.
Second field. Same pattern: cin >> list[i].fieldname; for every field in the struct.
Third field. One cin per field, same order as the struct definition.
KEY: Pattern is void read_X(Type &list[], int count) { for(i<count) { cin >> list[i].field; per field } }.

spec: Q3 sim - write void read_computers(computer_data &list[], int count) that reads id, description, location for each

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Signature is void read_X(Type &list[], int count). void because it modifies through &, no return needed.
Standard counting loop: start at 0, go up to count-1, increment by 1.
Read the int field first. cin >> list[i].field reads one value from input into that struct's field.
Read each remaining field in order. One cin statement per field.
Last field. Every field in the struct gets its own cin >> line.
KEY: Pattern is void read_X(Type &list[], int count) { for loop { cin >> list[i].field; for each field } }.

spec: Q3 sim - write void read_students(student_data &list[], int count) that reads id, name, course for each
explanation: void read_X(Type &list[], int count) { for loop with cin >> list[i].field; }

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Signature always follows void read_X(Type &list[], int count). Name matches the entity.
for loop from 0 to count-1. This iterates through each array element to read into.
First field: cin >> list[i].fieldname; reads from standard input into the i-th struct's field.
Second field. Repeat cin >> list[i].field; for every field the struct has.
Third field. Match the struct's fields in order, one cin per field.
KEY: Pattern is void read_X(Type &list[], int count) { for(i<count) { one cin >> per field } }.

steps.annotation: Every program needs int main() as the entry point
return 0 at the end signals the program completed successfully
int main() is the required entry point for every C++ program
End main with return 0 to tell the OS everything finished normally
STRATEGY: main() always follows the same skeleton: declare constants, declare array, read count, call read function, print loop, return 0.
Declare const MAX first. This sets the maximum array size. Always 100 for exam questions.
Declare the struct array using Type list[MAX]. Uses the const MAX just declared.
Read the actual count from input. This tells us how many items to process (always less than MAX).
Call the read function passing list and count. No & needed here -- arrays already pass by reference.
KEY: Pattern is const MAX, Type list[MAX], cin count, call read_X(list,count), printf loop with .c_str() for strings, return 0.

", ...)

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: main() skeleton: const MAX, array declaration, int count, cin count, call read, print loop, return 0. Always this order.
const int MAX = 100; -- this never changes. Declare it before the array that uses it.
Declare array of structs: Type list[MAX]. Use the entity's struct type.
Call read function: read_X(list, count). Pass the array and count directly -- no & needed for arrays.
printf uses %d for int, %s for string. Every string field needs .c_str() to convert to C-string.
KEY: Pattern is int main() { const MAX=100; Type list[MAX]; cin>>count; read_X(list,count); printf loop with .c_str(); return 0; }.

explanation: return 0 signals successful program completion

explanation: int main() { — empty parentheses (no parameters) then opening brace

steps.annotation: This mock exam struct has three double fields: mystery, a, and b
who_am_i takes a struct by reference and computes mystery
mystery is set to the sum of a and b; this is a simple addition pattern
The struct holds mystery plus two input values x and y
mystery stores the product of x and y; a straightforward multiplication pattern
STRATEGY: Trace who_am_i by tracking mystery. It starts as numbers[0] = 2.5.
Loop runs with i = 1, 2, 3, 4. Each iteration compares numbers[i] against current mystery.
i=1: 8.3 > 2.5 is true, so mystery becomes 8.3. i=2: 1.0 > 8.3 is false, skip. i=3: 6.6 > 8.3 is false, skip. i=4: 4.9 > 8.3 is false, skip.
Only executes when the if-condition is true. mystery updates to the new larger value.
KEY: This is a max-finder. Init with [0], loop 1..4, keep the biggest. Final mystery = 8.3.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Q1 who_am_i always has signature void who_am_i(stat_double &data). The & is required for pass-by-reference.

That's it -- one line of body. No loop needed for simple two-field formulas.
KEY: Pattern is void who_am_i(stat_double &data) { data.mystery = formula using data.fields; }.

Compare each element to current max; update mystery when larger found

Update mystery whenever a smaller element is found; this finds the minimum

Loop checks i = 1, 2, 3, 4. Each compares numbers[i] to the current mystery value.
mystery updates only when a bigger value is found. It keeps the running maximum.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Q1 who_am_i with array uses the loop pattern. Signature is always void who_am_i(stat_double &data).
Initialize mystery with numbers[0]. This is the starting value for comparison.
Loop from 1 to 4 (skip index 0 since we already used it). Compares remaining elements.
This example finds min (uses <). Your card finds max (uses >). Only the comparison operator differs.
Update mystery when condition is true. Body is the same for min or max.
KEY: Pattern is init mystery=[0], for i=1..4, if numbers[i] >/< mystery then update. Change < to > for max.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Accumulator pattern for who_am_i. Signature is always void who_am_i(stat_double &data).
Initialize mystery to the identity value. For product it's 1. For sum (your card) it's 0.
Loop from 0 to 4 -- accumulator patterns start at 0 because every element contributes.
Accumulate: mystery = mystery op numbers[i]. This uses *. Your card uses + instead.
KEY: Accumulator pattern: init mystery to identity (0 for sum, 1 for product), loop all 5, accumulate with operator.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Q3 read function for stat_double. Same signature pattern: void read_stat(stat_double &list[], int count).

Inner loop iterates over the 5 elements of the array field. Use j (not i) for the inner index.
Read into list[i].arrayfield[j]. Two indices: i for which struct, j for which element in the array.
KEY: Nested read pattern: outer for(i<count), inner for(j<5), cin >> list[i].arrayfield[j];.

steps.annotation: Check which value is larger to avoid a negative result
If a > b, subtract b from a
Otherwise subtract a from b; mystery is always the absolute difference
Assume x is the larger value to start with
Check whether y beats x; if so, mystery becomes y instead
mystery ends up holding the larger of x and y; a max of two pattern
STRATEGY: Trace who_am_i by tracking mystery. Starts as numbers[0] = 7.7.
Loop checks i = 1, 2, 3, 4 against current mystery.
Only one update this time: when 9.9 exceeded 7.7. Mystery stays at 9.9 after that.
KEY: Max-finder: init with [0], loop 1..4, update when bigger. Final mystery = 9.9.

teachMe: Mock exam ME-04 Q1: trace who_am_i. Final mystery = 3.0.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Absolute difference needs an if/else to avoid negative results. Same who_am_i signature.
Check which field is larger. If x > y, subtract y from x. This walkthrough uses x,y; your card uses a,b.
When x is bigger: mystery = x - y. Result is positive because x > y.
When y is bigger (or equal): mystery = y - x. Also positive because y >= x.
KEY: Absolute difference pattern: if(a>b) mystery=a-b; else mystery=b-a; -- always subtract smaller from larger.

spec: Q1 sim (mock 4): write void who_am_i(stat_double &data) that sets mystery to absolute difference of a and b
explanation: Q1 abs diff: if a>b then a-b else b-a.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Q3 read function. Signature is void read_stat(stat_double &list[], int count). Same every time.
Loop from 0 to count-1. One iteration per struct in the array.
Read first field: cin >> list[i].field; This uses x,y; your card uses a,b. Same pattern.
Read second field. One cin >> per field, matching the struct's non-mystery fields.
KEY: Pattern is for(i<count) { cin >> list[i].field; per non-mystery field }. No nested loop needed for plain fields.

teachMe: a=7.5, b=3.2. a>b is true, so mystery = 7.5 - 3.2 = 4.3
a=2.0, b=8.5. a>b is false, so else branch: mystery = 8.5 - 2.0 = 6.5
a=5.0, b=5.0. a>b is false (equal, not greater), so else: mystery = 5.0 - 5.0 = 0.0
a=1.3, b=9.7. a>b is false, so else: mystery = 9.7 - 1.3 = 8.4

clozeSentence: Check if a is ___ (greater than) b to decide which subtraction order
explanation: If a > b, subtract b from a. Otherwise subtract a from b. Either way result is positive.

clozeSentence: The ___ branch handles when b >= a
explanation: else runs when the if condition is false (a is not greater than b)

clozeSentence: In the else branch, subtract the smaller from the larger: ___ - ___
explanation: When a <= b, we compute b - a to get a positive result (absolute difference)

spec: Write who_am_i that sets mystery to the absolute difference |a - b| using if/else
explanation: if a>b: mystery=a-b, else: mystery=b-a. No abs() needed -- just swap subtraction order.

spec: Write who_am_i that sets mystery to |a - b|. Include the struct definition.
explanation: struct has double a, double b, double mystery. who_am_i uses if/else to ensure positive difference.

explanation: Without absolute value, it is a one-liner like ME-01. No if/else needed.

steps.annotation: Initialize counter to 0 before counting
Check if each element is positive
Add 1 for each positive number; mystery ends as the count of positives
Start the counter at zero before scanning the array
Test each element for a condition (negative in this case)
Increment by 1 for each match; mystery ends as the count of negatives
STRATEGY: Trace who_am_i by tracking mystery. Starts as numbers[0] = 11.1.
Loop checks i = 1, 2, 3, 4 against current mystery.
Two updates this time: at i=1 and i=2. After i=2, mystery=33.3 and nothing beats it.
KEY: Max-finder: init with [0], loop 1..4, update when bigger. Final mystery = 33.3.

teachMe: Mock exam ME-05 Q1: trace who_am_i. Final mystery = 7.7.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Counter pattern for who_am_i. Same signature: void who_am_i(stat_double &data).
Initialize counter to 0. Counting always starts from zero.
Loop from 0 to 4 -- check every element. Counter patterns always start at 0.
This counts negatives (< 0). Your card counts positives (> 0). Only the comparison changes.
Increment by 1 when condition is true. mystery = mystery + 1 is the counting step.
KEY: Counter pattern: init mystery=0, loop all 5, if condition then mystery=mystery+1. Change < 0 to > 0 for positive count.

spec: Q1 sim (mock 5): write void who_am_i(stat_double &data) that sets mystery to count of positive numbers in numbers[0..4]
explanation: Q1 count pattern: init 0, loop all, if condition met increment.

steps.annotation: STRATEGY: Q4 main follows the standard skeleton: const MAX, array, count, cin, call read, return 0.
Always declare const int MAX = 100 first. This caps the array size.
Declare the array: stat_double list[MAX]; Uses the struct type from Q2.
Read the actual count from input. Declare int count on the line before this.
Call read_stat(list, count). Pass array and count directly -- no & needed for arrays.
KEY: Pattern is int main() { const MAX=100; stat_double list[MAX]; cin>>count; read_stat(list,count); return 0; }.

teachMe: init: 0. All values are <= 0 (negatives and zero). 0.0 is NOT > 0, so no increments. Final: 0.0 (zero positives)
init: 0. All five values are positive. Every iteration increments. Final: 5.0 (all five positive)
partial trace -- value at i=3 BEFORE iteration runs. init: 0. i=0: -5.5>0? no. i=1: 2.3>0 yes->1. i=2: 0.0>0? no (zero is not positive). At i=3 entry mystery is 1.0. Full run: i=3 no, i=4 yes->2.0.

steps.annotation: The signature shape: returnType funcName(type &paramName). The & lives between int and x.
Inside the body, x is just a name. No special syntax needed for references.

steps.annotation: & parameter requires a real variable as the argument.

steps.annotation: Legal: counter has an address; x can alias it.

steps.annotation: Illegal: 7 is a literal, no address. Compiler refuses.
After inc(counter): counter = 1.

steps.code: int n;          // declare a variable first
steps.annotation: MUST declare a real variable before calling a &-taking function.

steps.code: store(n);       // pass that variable by reference

store wrote 42 into n's box. Output: 42.
a is a real variable. Legal to bind reference to it.

steps.annotation: a + b is a transient value (3). It has no fixed home. Reference cannot bind.
After f(a): a was overwritten to 99. Output: 99.

balance was declared before the call. Reference parameter binds; x = 0 zeroes balance.

steps.annotation: struct keyword introduces a NAMED bundle of related fields. Here the bundle is called 'book'.
First field: a string named title. One field per line.
Close brace AND semicolon end the struct definition.
Now book is a usable type. novel is one bundle of three fields.

levelLabel: L11 -> related fields under one name

steps.annotation: A struct gathers logically related fields under one name -- here 'book'.
Each field has its type and name. They all live inside one book together.
b is a book -- it has a title field AND a pages field, bundled together.
struct book is a TYPE. It is not yet a value -- it's a recipe for values.
one is the first instance of book.
two is a second, independent instance.
Each book has its own title field. one and two are separate.

levelLabel: L11 -> definition makes a new type

steps.annotation: Defining the type. employee_data is a TEMPLATE/SHAPE, not a value.
After the type exists, you can instantiate as many as you want.
Each instance has its own copy of all fields, totally independent.
The closing brace must be followed by a semicolon. } alone = compile error.
After }; the type is registered. Code below can declare instances.
